---
title: "Introducing The Athletic’s season-level player dashboard to compare and contrast profiles"
source: "https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/7243023/2026/05/05/season-level-player-dashboard-the-athletic/"
author:
  - "[[Mark Carey]]"
published: 2026-05-05
created: 2026-05-10
description: "We already have match-level analysis for players across 90 minutes - now we can show their style aggregated across the season"
tags:
  - "clippings"
---
Alex Caparros/Getty Images

Player profiling has never been more popular in football.

Even a casual analyst knows that searching for a new centre-forward by their position alone is a blunt method to filter from. Distinguishing an out-and-out poacher from a roaming, back-to-goal No 9 is where you can truly focus your scouting resources.

Data can be a helpful tool when sharpening that focus. Statistics themselves are the raw material, but using those numbers to generate meaningful insight is where things begin to get interesting.

Daily Pick'em

Serie A

20:45

Tonight

71% Picked

<100 votes

29% Picked

Last year, *The Athletic* released new [‘player style’ pizza charts to provide a statistical breakdown of a player’s profile](https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/6457393/2025/08/07/player-pizza-charts-version-two-2025/) when attacking, defending, possessing, and progressing the ball. The rise of football analytics means that many fans are familiar with these pizza-chart visualisations, and they are an intuitive way to break down a player’s style.

However, can we go further? Can we create something more detailed, something more… novel?

Well, yes. So let us introduce *The Athletic* ’s new season-level player dashboard.

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04034612/Arda-Guler_player_roles_2025-26.png)

Our data visualisation menu already includes a [player dashboard at the match level](https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/5860669/2024/10/22/the-athletic-player-dashboard-explainer-rashford-kane/), outlining a player’s individual actions on the pitch and how it compares with their peers within a single 90 minutes.

Here, we take a zoomed-out view and look at a player’s style aggregated across the season.

You will notice there are not lots of numbers plastered onto the visualisation, and that is by design. We are here to answer the football questions. Where does a player take their touches? What role do they play in their team? Who plays most similarly to them?

Fuelled by data from Opta and SkillCorner, but explained in football language, this dashboard provides you with a one-stop shop for all your recruitment and scouting needs.

Let’s walk through what each section represents.

---

Starting with the simple — but no less important — descriptive information.

A quick scan over a player’s age, footedness, and minutes played can be hugely revealing from a recruitment perspective. The best ability is availability, after all.

Below the descriptive information is a headline explanation of the player’s role in the team. As shown with Real Madrid’s Arda Guler’s above, a player can nominally play multiple positions in different formations, but the revealing insight is what their skill set can bring to the party.

For Paris Saint-Germain’s Vitinha, our analysis profiles the Portugal international as the “Tempo Controller” — a player who gets on the ball more than most to set the tone of the game from midfield.

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04035219/Vitinha_player_roles_2025-26.png)

The analysis that sits behind this is far more complex than the single line representing it.

[Building on *The Athletic’* s previous work by John Muller](https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/3473297/2022/08/10/player-roles-the-athletic/), a player’s role is calculated using nearly 40 metrics for nearly 3,000 players across Europe’s top five leagues.

These players were entered into a statistical model (across the 2023-24 and 2024-25 seasons) that identifies patterns in the metrics to bucket those who perform similarly in their style. Performing this analysis (across two levels) provides us with different buckets (or clusters, in statistical terms) that exist among Europe’s players. Naming those buckets into meaningful player roles is the crucial stage.

That training model is then used to predict the roles of each player in our 2025-26 dataset. From ‘Ball-playing Defenders’ to ‘Playmakers’, this analysis helps to focus the mind when looking through a player’s dashboard.

---

Based on the role that they play, the next thing to catch your eye is a short breakdown of a player’s style compared with their peers across Europe.

This is calculated using a “percentile” rating between zero and 99 for each metric. The higher the number, the more frequently — or efficiently — the player performs this action compared with others within their cohort.

To save you the bother of separating a 72 from a 75 out of 99 rating, these percentiles are simplified into five buckets — with players’ output falling between 0-20, 21-40, 41-60, 61-80, and 81-99.

As an example, Manchester City’s Rayan Cherki is heavily active in possession when his side goes forward (“on-ball involvement”), with an ability to look after the ball when opponents get close to him (“press resistance”).

With a gifted dribbling ability, Cherki’s tendency to bring the ball forward is also among the most prolific in Europe (“carry progression”), although the analysis captures Pep Guardiola’s reservation that he might not be the most active off the ball compared with his peers (“front-foot defending”).

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04035205/Cherki_style.png)

With an assist (and expected assist) rate that is among the best in Europe, the 22-year-old’s “creative threat” ranks highly, which triggers the next graphic.

This graphic (top right on the dashboard) is bespoke to the player in question, depending on their identified role in the analysis. Given Cherki’s 11 open-play assists at the time of writing, his dashboard spits out a “chances created” graphic to show where he has provided for his team-mates.

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04171847/Rayan-Cherki_player_roles_2025-26.png)

---

A player’s role provides the clearest picture of their responsibilities for their team, but position names can still be a decent yardstick to show where their manager has deployed them.

Therefore, the dashboard includes a player’s share of minutes within each position, dovetailed with the specific locations of their touches within the season. This provides an extra layer of detail to outline the zones they occupy most frequently as part of their team setup.

For example, Declan Rice is nominally a central or defensive midfielder on paper, but his heatmap emphasises that his influence comes in the left halfspace — galloping up and down the pitch to support Arsenal’s build-up before impacting their attack.

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04035225/Declan-Rice_player_roles_2025-26.png)

When trying to explain a player’s style in simple terms, a common shortcut is to start the sentence with “they play a bit like…” We do this analysis in our head, using our own football knowledge — but what if we could quantify it more accurately?

The dashboard provides a simple analysis to show the top 10 players whose actions closely resemble the player in question. It does not specify which side of the pitch they operate, but focuses on the underlying skill set they offer.

With Liverpool tasked with trying to replace Mohamed Salah this season, plenty of attention has swirled around RB Leipzig winger, Yan Diomande. As reported by *The Athletic i* n January, [the 19-year-old’s asking price would be close to €100million](https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/6980553/2026/01/20/transfer-news-premier-league-latest/) — meaning Liverpool could get priced out should they wish to secure his signature. So, which players in Europe profile similarly?

While the likes of Vinicius Junior, Bukayo Saka and Lamine Yamal are unrealistic, Real Sociedad’s Takefusa Kubo is an alternative option who could be attractive at a more affordable price. Meanwhile, Atalanta’s Charles De Ketelaere is more of a ball-to-feet player, but provides a similar left-footed option to come in from the right wing.

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04035230/Yan-Diomande_player_roles_2025-26.png)

*The Athletic’* s player dashboard is designed to give you instant insight into every player across Europe’s top five leagues.

Knowing the playing style of elite players in world football such as Harry Kane, Erling Haaland or Lamine Yamal is a little more straightforward, but what if we wanted to understand more about Rayo Vallecano’s left-back? This analysis can help to fill in any blind spots you might have.

Pep Chavarria, for those who were wondering…

![](https://static01.nyt.com/athletic/uploads/wp/2026/05/04034621/Pep-Chavarria_player_roles_2025-26.png)
